title: How to Obtain a 'VAT Number' as a Freelancer in the Netherlands

interlinking_suggestions.title: The Ultimate Digital Nomad Tax Survival Guide for 2025
Can Digital Nomads Claim the Home Office Deduction?
A Guide to the Netherlands' 'Eenmanszaak' for Sole Traders

author.name: Maria Kowalski
author.bio: Based in Berlin, Maria helps non-EU freelancers navigate the complexities of the European market. She's an expert on VAT, EU-specific invoicing requirements, and business registration across different EU countries.

reviewedBy.name: Dr. Alistair Finch
reviewedBy.bio: With a Ph.D. in Economics and over 15 years at a Big Four accounting firm, Alistair specializes in demystifying cross-border tax law for independent professionals. He focuses on risk mitigation and long-term financial planning.

key_takeaways: Opt for the standard VAT regime over the Small Business Scheme (KOR) to reclaim significant VAT on business expenses and project a professional image to corporate clients.
Protect your personal information by using your public `btw-id` on all invoices and reserving your private `omzetbelastingnummer` exclusively for tax authority communications.
Before invoicing any EU-based business, you must validate their VAT number using the official VIES database to legally apply the 'VAT reverse-charge' mechanism.
Immediately transfer all VAT collected from client payments into a separate 'VAT Vault' bank account to eliminate cash-flow problems when quarterly tax payments are due.
summary: Global professionals in the Netherlands often face anxiety over cross-border tax compliance, a problem made worse by the financial drawbacks of the Small Business Scheme (KOR). This framework advises high-earners to instead register for the standard VAT regime, master the secure two-number system for invoicing, and correctly apply the reverse-charge mechanism for EU clients. By implementing these systems, you eliminate compliance risks, gain financial control, and establish a resilient foundation to operate confidently as the CEO of your international business.
ai_overview_answer: To get a Dutch VAT number, you must register your business with the Dutch Chamber of Commerce (KVK), which automatically triggers the tax registration process with the `Belastingdienst` (Dutch Tax and Customs Administration). After your KVK appointment, the tax office will mail you two distinct numbers: a public VAT identification number (`btw-id`) for your invoices and a private turnover tax number (`omzetbelastingnummer`) for official correspondence. Professionals earning over €20,000 annually should choose the standard VAT regime over the Small Business Scheme (KOR) to be able to reclaim VAT on business expenses. To register, you will need a valid ID, proof of a Dutch address, your BSN (citizen service number), and a payment card. For invoicing EU business clients, you must verify their VAT ID via the VIES system and apply the "VAT reverse-charged" mechanism. For clients outside the EU, you do not charge Dutch VAT, but you must note the service location on the invoice. Finally, it is critical to manage collected VAT by transferring it to a separate bank account to prepare for quarterly tax payments.

itemListElement.name: How to Obtain a 'VAT Number' as a Freelancer in the Netherlands

@graph.headline: How to Obtain a 'VAT Number' as a Freelancer in the Netherlands
@graph.description: Global professionals in the Netherlands often face anxiety over cross-border tax compliance, a problem made worse by the financial drawbacks of the Small Business Scheme (KOR). This framework advises high-earners to instead register for the standard VAT regime, master the secure two-number system for invoicing, and correctly apply the reverse-charge mechanism for EU clients. By implementing these systems, you eliminate compliance risks, gain financial control, and establish a resilient foundation to operate confidently as the CEO of your international business.

step.name: Step 1: Is the Small Business Scheme (KOR) a Trap for High-Earners?
step.text: The peace of mind we just mentioned begins with your first strategic decision: how you engage with the Dutch tax system. Many guides point new freelancers toward the Small Business Scheme (`kleineondernemersregeling`, or KOR). On the surface, its VAT exemption seems attractive. For a high-earning global professional, however, this shortcut can quickly become a strategic trap. The KOR is a VAT exemption for businesses with an annual turnover of €20,000 or less in the Netherlands. If you opt in, you don’t charge VAT on your invoices and don’t have to file regular VAT returns. For a local, part-time artisan, this makes sense. For you, it introduces trade-offs that directly impact your bottom line and professional standing. The most critical consequence of the KOR is that you cannot reclaim the VAT you pay on your business expenses. For a high-growth Business-of-One, these unrecoverable costs can amount to thousands of euros per year. By choosing the standard VAT regime, you treat this "input tax" as a temporary cash flow item that the `Belastingdienst` (Dutch Tax and Customs Administration) refunds to you. Under the KOR, it becomes a permanent business expense. Beyond the financial drawback, operating without a VAT number sends a subtle signal to corporate clients. In the B2B world, a VAT registration is standard procedure. It shows you are a legitimate entity prepared for professional engagement. Lacking a VAT number can inadvertently position you as a "small-time" operator, creating friction with procurement departments accustomed to clean, VAT-compliant invoicing. For the global professional whose ambitions far exceed the €20,000 threshold, the verdict is clear. Bypassing the KOR and embracing the standard VAT regime is the superior strategic path. It optimizes your finances, strengthens your credibility, and establishes a scalable foundation for your tax compliance from day one.

step.name: Step 2: Securing Your Foundation—KVK Registration & Your Two Critical Numbers
step.text: Having made the strategic decision to operate under the standard VAT regime, your next move is to lay the formal cornerstone for your business. This isn’t just paperwork; it's about forging your professional identity in the Netherlands and establishing a security protocol to shield your personal information. This pivotal step happens at the Dutch Chamber of Commerce (`Kamer van Koophandel`, or KVK). Registering your business with the KVK is the non-negotiable first action to formalize your operations. This single appointment establishes your `eenmanszaak` (sole proprietorship) and automatically notifies the `Belastingdienst` to begin your tax registration. Within two weeks, they will mail you two separate letters, each containing an essential number. Mastering their distinct purposes is vital for your operational security. Since 2020, the Netherlands has operated a two-number system to protect the privacy of sole proprietors by severing the link between public business identity and the private citizen service number (`BSN`). Confusing them is a common and preventable error. Your time is your most valuable asset. Arrive at your scheduled appointment prepared and organized. Completing this registration solidifies your business's legal foundation. You will leave the KVK office with your official KVK number, and the process to secure your crucial `btw-id` will be underway.

step.name: Step 3: The Cross-Border Invoicing Masterclass
step.text: With your legal foundation in place and your `btw-id` on its way, you are ready to engage the global market. Invoicing clients across borders is a critical risk management function. The core principle for a Netherlands-based service provider is simple: you generally do not charge Dutch VAT to business clients outside the Netherlands. Instead, you shift this responsibility to them using a mechanism called the "reverse-charge." When providing services to a business in another EU member state, the process is precise. Each step creates documentation that protects you from future tax liability. Engaging with clients outside the European Union, such as in the United States or the United Kingdom, is administratively simpler but still requires careful documentation. This reinforces the importance of keeping clear records and contracts that prove your client is based outside the EU.

step.name: Step 4: Beyond Compliance—A System for Control and Cash Flow
step.text: Correct invoicing is half the equation; the other half is building an internal system that transforms VAT from a source of anxiety into a tool for financial control. This is how you move from being compliant to operating with CEO-level financial clarity. Set Up a "VAT Vault" Bank Account. Master VAT Reclamation. Systematize Your Quarterly Returns (`btw-aangifte`). The Annual VAT Return Myth. Knowing this removes the phantom stress of a non-existent annual deadline.

mainEntity.name: Do I charge VAT to a US client from the Netherlands?

acceptedAnswer.text: No. For B2B services provided to a client outside the European Union, the "place of supply" is the client's location. You do not charge Dutch VAT. Your invoice should state: "0% VAT, service supplied outside the EU."

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between a `btw-id` and an `ob-nummer`?

acceptedAnswer.text:
This distinction is critical for your privacy.
- `btw-id`: Your public VAT number for invoices and client communication.
- `ob-nummer`: Your private tax number, linked to your BSN, used *only* for communication with the Dutch tax office (`Belastingdienst`). Never publish it.

mainEntity.name: How do I use the VAT reverse-charge in the Netherlands?

acceptedAnswer.text: When invoicing a business in another EU country, you don't charge Dutch VAT. Instead, you shift the responsibility to the client. Your invoice must include their valid EU VAT number and the phrase "VAT reverse-charged" (and ideally, `btw verlegd`).

mainEntity.name: How do I verify a European client's VAT number?

acceptedAnswer.text: You must use the official European Commission VIES (VAT Information Exchange System) website. This free, instant check is a non-negotiable step before issuing a reverse-charged invoice. Screen-shot the validation for your records.

mainEntity.name: Do I need a Dutch bank account to get a VAT number?

acceptedAnswer.text: While not a direct prerequisite for the VAT number itself, a Dutch bank account (IBAN) is an operational necessity. You will need it to pay the KVK registration fee and to manage your business finances effectively within the Netherlands.

mainEntity.name: What are the current VAT rates in the Netherlands?

acceptedAnswer.text:
The Netherlands primarily uses three rates:
- 21% Standard Rate: Applies to most goods and services.
- 9% Reduced Rate: Applies to specific items like books, foodstuffs, and certain artistic services.
- 0% Rate: Applies to cross-border transactions, such as services to businesses outside the Netherlands.

body.content: How to Get Your Dutch VAT Number: The Global Professional's Framework

You’re not just a freelancer in the Netherlands; you're the CEO of a global "Business-of-One." Your primary challenge isn't finding high-value clients—it's managing the subtle but persistent anxiety of cross-border compliance. The fear of a misstep on an invoice or a surprise tax liability is a significant distraction from the strategic work that drives your growth.

This guide moves beyond generic government checklists. It is a strategic framework designed to help you secure your Dutch VAT registration and, more importantly, master the international invoicing system with complete confidence. Our goal isn't just to get you a number; it's to build a resilient operational system.

Together, we will construct a bulletproof invoicing process that protects your business and satisfies tax authorities. By the end, you will have a clear framework to eliminate compliance risks, giving you the peace of mind to operate as the global professional you are.
Step 1: Is the Small Business Scheme (KOR) a Trap for High-Earners?

The peace of mind we just mentioned begins with your first strategic decision: how you engage with the Dutch tax system. Many guides point new freelancers toward the Small Business Scheme (`kleineondernemersregeling`, or KOR). On the surface, its VAT exemption seems attractive. For a high-earning global professional, however, this shortcut can quickly become a strategic trap.

The KOR is a VAT exemption for businesses with an annual turnover of €20,000 or less in the Netherlands. If you opt in, you don’t charge VAT on your invoices and don’t have to file regular VAT returns. For a local, part-time artisan, this makes sense. For you, it introduces trade-offs that directly impact your bottom line and professional standing.
The High-Earner's Trade-Off: Leaving Money on the Table
The most critical consequence of the KOR is that you **cannot reclaim the VAT** you pay on your business expenses. Consider the essential tools of your trade:

items: A new €2,500 MacBook Pro has 21% VAT baked into the price—over €430 you cannot recover.
Monthly subscriptions to Adobe Creative Cloud, Figma, and Notion all include VAT.
Co-working space memberships and professional accounting services are subject to VAT.

body.content: For a high-growth Business-of-One, these unrecoverable costs can amount to thousands of euros per year. By choosing the standard VAT regime, you treat this "input tax" as a temporary cash flow item that the `Belastingdienst` (Dutch Tax and Customs Administration) refunds to you. Under the KOR, it becomes a permanent business expense.

rows: May signal a small-scale or beginner operation

body.content: The Professionalism Signal: Perception is Reality

Beyond the financial drawback, operating without a VAT number sends a subtle signal to corporate clients. In the B2B world, a VAT registration is standard procedure. It shows you are a legitimate entity prepared for professional engagement. Lacking a VAT number can inadvertently position you as a "small-time" operator, creating friction with procurement departments accustomed to clean, VAT-compliant invoicing.

For the global professional whose ambitions far exceed the €20,000 threshold, the verdict is clear. Bypassing the KOR and embracing the standard VAT regime is the superior strategic path. It optimizes your finances, strengthens your credibility, and establishes a scalable foundation for your tax compliance from day one.
Step 2: Securing Your Foundation—KVK Registration & Your Two Critical Numbers

Having made the strategic decision to operate under the standard VAT regime, your next move is to lay the formal cornerstone for your business. This isn’t just paperwork; it's about forging your professional identity in the Netherlands and establishing a security protocol to shield your personal information. This pivotal step happens at the Dutch Chamber of Commerce (`Kamer van Koophandel`, or KVK).

Registering your business with the KVK is the non-negotiable first action to formalize your operations. This single appointment establishes your `eenmanszaak` (sole proprietorship) and automatically notifies the `Belastingdienst` to begin your tax registration. Within two weeks, they will mail you two separate letters, each containing an essential number. Mastering their distinct purposes is vital for your operational security.
The Two-Number System: A Critical Security Protocol

Since 2020, the Netherlands has operated a two-number system to protect the privacy of sole proprietors by severing the link between public business identity and the private citizen service number (`BSN`). Confusing them is a common and preventable error.

rows: **Public & Commercial.** For all external communication with clients and suppliers.
**Private & Administrative.** For your direct communication with the `Belastingdienst` only.
On all invoices, your website, email signatures, and quotes.
When filing VAT returns or in any correspondence with the tax authorities.
Starts with **NL**, 9 random digits, 'B', and a 2-digit control number (e.g., NL000099998B57).
Your BSN, followed by 'B' and a 2 or 3-digit code (e.g., 111234567B01).
None. This number is designed to be public.
**Never** put this on a public document. Doing so is the digital equivalent of publishing your passport number.

body.content: Actionable Checklist for a Smooth KVK Appointment
Your time is your most valuable asset. Arrive at your scheduled appointment prepared and organized.

items: **Valid Identification:** A passport is the standard, especially for non-EU citizens.
**Proof of Dutch Address:** A signed rental or purchase contract for your home address is sufficient if you work from home. A physical address in the Netherlands is required.
**Your BSN (`burgerservicenummer`):** Your personal citizen service number.
**Clear Business Description:** Be ready to describe your primary business activities in a single, concise sentence and select an official business code (SBI code).
**Payment Card:** You must pay a one-time registration fee (currently €82.25). Cash is not accepted.

body.content: Completing this registration solidifies your business's legal foundation. You will leave the KVK office with your official KVK number, and the process to secure your crucial `btw-id` will be underway.
Step 3: The Cross-Border Invoicing Masterclass

With your legal foundation in place and your `btw-id` on its way, you are ready to engage the global market. Invoicing clients across borders is a critical risk management function. The core principle for a Netherlands-based service provider is simple: you generally do not charge Dutch VAT to business clients outside the Netherlands. Instead, you shift this responsibility to them using a mechanism called the "reverse-charge."
Invoicing an EU-Based Business Client (e.g., Germany, France)
When providing services to a business in another EU member state, the process is precise. Each step creates documentation that protects you from future tax liability.

items: **Verify Their VAT ID—Your First Line of Defense.** Before drafting the invoice, you **must** verify your client's VAT number using the European Commission's official VIES (VAT Information Exchange System) website. This is not optional. An unverified number can invalidate the reverse-charge, potentially making you liable for the unpaid VAT. Your VIES validation screenshot is your proof of due diligence.

**State "VAT Reverse-Charged" on the Invoice.** Do not add 21% Dutch VAT to the total. Instead, include your client's verified VAT number and the specific phrase **"VAT reverse-charged."** For clarity, it is best practice to also include the Dutch phrase: **`btw verlegd`**. This statement legally shifts the VAT obligation to your client.

**File a Separate ICP Declaration.** In addition to your regular quarterly VAT return, you must file a separate declaration called the `Opgaaf intracommunautaire prestaties` (ICP). This report, filed with the `Belastingdienst`, lists the total value of services provided to each EU client, identified by their VAT number. This allows tax authorities across the EU to cross-reference transactions.

body.content: Invoicing a US or UK-Based Business Client
Engaging with clients outside the European Union, such as in the United States or the United Kingdom, is administratively simpler but still requires careful documentation.

items: **No Dutch VAT Charged.** Services provided to business clients located outside the EU are considered "out of scope" for Dutch VAT. You do not charge any VAT on these invoices.

**Document the Service Location.** Your invoice must justify the absence of VAT. To create an unambiguous paper trail, include a statement such as **"0% VAT rate, services supplied outside the EU"** or **"Service location: USA/UK."** This demonstrates your correct application of international tax rules.

**No VIES, No ICP.** Because these transactions fall outside the EU's VAT territory, you do not need to use the VIES system or file an ICP declaration. This reinforces the importance of keeping clear records and contracts that prove your client is based outside the EU.

body.content: Step 4: Beyond Compliance—A System for Control and Cash Flow

Correct invoicing is half the equation; the other half is building an internal system that transforms VAT from a source of anxiety into a tool for financial control. This is how you move from being compliant to operating with CEO-level financial clarity.

items: **Set Up a "VAT Vault" Bank Account.** This is the single most powerful discipline you can adopt. The moment a client pays an invoice that includes VAT, immediately transfer the full VAT amount into a separate business bank account. This "VAT Vault" is not your money; you are simply holding it for the `Belastingdienst`. This act of segregation eliminates the risk of accidentally spending tax funds and prevents the cash-flow shock that cripples many freelancers when their quarterly payment is due.

**Master VAT Reclamation.** As a VAT-registered professional, you are entitled to reclaim the VAT you pay on legitimate business expenses. This is a direct financial advantage of operating outside the KOR. You can reclaim VAT on critical investments like hardware, software subscriptions, professional services, and work-related travel. To do this flawlessly, you must keep meticulous records, ensuring every expense is documented with a proper business invoice that includes your VAT number.

**Systematize Your Quarterly Returns (`btw-aangifte`).** For most freelancers, VAT returns are filed quarterly. Do not let this deadline create stress. Use modern accounting software (like Moneybird or Xero) that can automate most of the reporting. Block a recurring 90-minute appointment on your calendar for the last week of every quarter and treat it with the same seriousness as a client meeting. This transforms your `btw-aangifte` from a last-minute scramble into a routine operational checkpoint.

**The Annual VAT Return Myth.** Many professionals incorrectly assume there is a mandatory annual VAT return. For most freelancers, this is not the case. The form known as the `suppletie omzetbelasting` is a **correction form**, not a standard annual filing. It is used to amend errors made in previous quarterly filings from the past five years. If you discover you've misreported VAT by more than €1,000, you are obligated to file a `suppletie`. Knowing this removes the phantom stress of a non-existent annual deadline.

body.content: Frequently Asked Questions
This section provides clear, direct answers to the most common scenarios you'll face as a global professional operating from the Netherlands.
**Do I charge VAT to a US client from the Netherlands?**

No. For B2B services provided to a client outside the European Union, the "place of supply" is the client's location. You do not charge Dutch VAT. Your invoice should state: **"0% VAT, service supplied outside the EU."**
**What is the difference between a `btw-id` and an `ob-nummer`?**
This distinction is critical for your privacy.

items: **`btw-id`:** Your **public** VAT number for invoices and client communication.
**`ob-nummer`:** Your **private** tax number, linked to your BSN, used *only* for communication with the Dutch tax office (`Belastingdienst`). Never publish it.

body.content: **How do I use the VAT reverse-charge in the Netherlands?**

When invoicing a business in another EU country, you don't charge Dutch VAT. Instead, you shift the responsibility to the client. Your invoice must include their valid EU VAT number and the phrase **"VAT reverse-charged"** (and ideally, `btw verlegd`).
**How do I verify a European client's VAT number?**

You must use the official European Commission **VIES (VAT Information Exchange System)** website. This free, instant check is a non-negotiable step before issuing a reverse-charged invoice. Screen-shot the validation for your records.
**Do I need a Dutch bank account to get a VAT number?**

While not a direct prerequisite for the VAT number itself, a Dutch bank account (IBAN) is an operational necessity. You will need it to pay the KVK registration fee and to manage your business finances effectively within the Netherlands.
**What are the current VAT rates in the Netherlands?**
The Netherlands primarily uses three rates:

items: **21% Standard Rate:** Applies to most goods and services.
**9% Reduced Rate:** Applies to specific items like books, foodstuffs, and certain artistic services.
**0% Rate:** Applies to cross-border transactions, such as services to businesses outside the Netherlands.

body.content: Conclusion: From Anxious Freelancer to Confident CEO

Securing your Dutch VAT number marks a fundamental shift in your professional identity. You cease to be just a person doing freelance work and become the CEO of a legitimate, EU-based enterprise. This journey is the deliberate act of laying the foundation for a global business designed for resilience and growth.

Think back on the strategic choices you've made. Bypassing the Small Business Scheme (KOR) was a conscious investment in your high-growth trajectory. Registering with the KVK and mastering the two-number system was an act of risk management. Your invoicing playbook for EU and non-EU clients is now a robust operational standard that demonstrates international competence.

Finally, by turning compliance into a system—with a "VAT Vault" account and scheduled quarterly returns—you have seized control of your cash flow. You no longer need to fear the blue envelope from the `Belastingdienst`. You have a predictable, repeatable process that transforms tax obligations from a source of anxiety into a simple business task.

You are now equipped to operate your "Business-of-One" with the authority your clients expect. The goal was never just to get a number. It was to build a stable, professional platform from which you can confidently serve a global market. You are no longer a freelancer navigating the rules; you are a CEO in complete control of your Dutch-based international enterprise.
